config.question:
You are evaluating MiroFish — a multi-model AI decision engine.
Here is what MiroFish currently does:

ARCHITECTURE:
- 14+ LLM providers from 5 APIs (Gemini, Groq, DeepSeek, OpenAI, OpenRouter)
- Auto-discovery of available models via API scanning
- PMAgent orchestrates: dispatch → aggregate → debate → scale → synthesize
- Middleware chain: LoopDetection, ResponseDedup, ContextBudget, TimeoutGuard
- Per-model calibration weights by domain (correct +5%, wrong -5%)
- Conditional debate: triggered only when divergence >50% (ICML 2024)
- MoA synthesis: LLM aggregation for MEDIUM+ stakes decisions
- Skill library: 14 skills auto-matched to tasks, maturity tracking
- Agent memory: per-model experience logs
- Self-scaling: adds agents when consensus too low (<40%) or too high (>90%)

CURRENT WEAKNESSES (known):
- Memory is flat JSON files — no structure, no confidence scoring
- No streaming — all results batched at end
- Cost estimation stubbed out (always 0.0)
- No parallel tournament runs for CRITICAL decisions
- No graph-based orchestration (linear pipeline only)

YOUR TASK:
Evaluate each path below. For each, estimate the PERCENTAGE IMPROVEMENT
to overall decision quality, speed, or cost efficiency.

HARD CONSTRAINT: Only recommend paths where estimated improvement is >15%.
If a path gives <15% improvement, score it LOW on user_impact and dev_speed.
We want HIGH-ROI changes only, not incremental polish.

structured_memory.name: Structured Memory (Episodic/Semantic/Metric layers)
structured_memory.description: Replace flat JSON with 3-layer memory: episodic (decisions), semantic (extracted facts with confidence), metric (performance stats). Add MemoryEnrichmentMiddleware to auto-inject relevant facts into agent prompts.
structured_memory.best_case: Agents make 30% better decisions by accessing relevant past context. Calibration becomes automatic.
structured_memory.worst_case: Over-engineering for current scale. 2-3 days of work for marginal gain.

parallel_dispatch.name: True Parallel Dispatch with Per-Provider Timeouts
parallel_dispatch.description: Replace asyncio.gather() with asyncio.wait() + per-provider timeout. Fast providers (Groq 200ms) don't wait for slow ones (DeepSeek 3s). Process results as they arrive.
parallel_dispatch.best_case: 50% reduction in total latency for MEDIUM stakes (7-10 agents).
parallel_dispatch.worst_case: Minimal improvement if all providers are similarly fast.

cost_tracking.name: Real Token Counting + Cost Tracking
cost_tracking.description: Count actual tokens per agent call (via response metadata). Calculate real costs. Use cost data to optimize provider allocation (prefer cheaper models when quality is equal).
cost_tracking.best_case: 20-40% cost reduction by routing to cheapest-adequate model per task type.
cost_tracking.worst_case: Minimal savings since most providers are free tier.

decision_graph.name: DAG-based Decision Graph (mini-LangGraph)
decision_graph.description: Replace linear pipeline with directed graph: nodes = stages (dispatch, debate, synthesize, etc.), edges = conditional transitions. Enables skip-debate for high-consensus, parallel-committee for complex decisions.
decision_graph.best_case: More flexible orchestration. Can handle 3x more complex decision types.
decision_graph.worst_case: Heavy abstraction for simple decisions. Adds 500+ lines of code.

smart_early_exit.name: Smart Early Exit — Stop When Consensus Reached
smart_early_exit.description: After each agent returns, check if consensus already reached (e.g., 5/7 agree). Cancel remaining slow agents immediately. Return partial results.
smart_early_exit.best_case: 40-60% faster for easy decisions where consensus forms early.
smart_early_exit.worst_case: No improvement for contentious decisions where all agents needed.

concerns.structured_memory: Over-engineering for current scale may lead to unnecessary complexity.
concerns.parallel_dispatch: Potential for uneven load distribution among providers if not properly managed.
concerns.cost_tracking: Most providers being free tier may minimize cost savings.
concerns.decision_graph: Abstraction may be heavy for simple decisions, adding unnecessary complexity.
concerns.smart_early_exit: Early exit may not be effective for complex or contentious decisions.

agent_results.reason: Structured memory shows the highest potential for improving decision quality and user experience, with a 30% better decision rate and automatic calibration.

concerns.structured_memory: May lead to over-engineering for current scale, with marginal gain.
concerns.parallel_dispatch: Minimal improvement if all providers are similarly fast.
concerns.cost_tracking: Minimal savings since most providers are free tier.
concerns.decision_graph: Heavy abstraction for simple decisions, adding 500+ lines of code.
concerns.smart_early_exit: No improvement for contentious decisions where all agents are needed.

agent_results.reason: Parallel dispatch offers a significant reduction in total latency for MEDIUM stakes decisions, with a relatively low development effort.

concerns.structured_memory: breaking the model's ability to remember and retrieve context
concerns.parallel_dispatch: multiple calls and API usage variation for scalability
concerns.cost_tracking: not enough real cost data for precise cost tracking
concerns.decision_graph: potential UI interference with model recommendation
concerns.smart_early_exit: risk of premature skill replacement due to poor performance metrics

agent_results.reason: alternative in real-world scenarios, scalability will be crucial

concerns.structured_memory: Replacing flat JSON with layered memory adds schema complexity and may cause consistency bugs without proven need at current scale.
concerns.parallel_dispatch: Per‑provider timeouts could drop slower models that provide higher‑quality insights, risking decision quality.
concerns.cost_tracking: Token metadata varies across providers, leading to inaccurate cost estimates and suboptimal routing.
concerns.decision_graph: Graph orchestration adds substantial code complexity, increasing bug surface and maintenance overhead.
concerns.smart_early_exit: Early exit may discard minority but high‑quality opinions, degrading decisions on nuanced topics.

agent_results.reason: Offers ~50% latency cut with minimal code change and low risk, delivering fastest ROI.

concerns.structured_memory: The flat JSON memory is already a technical-debt bomb—adding a 3-layer schema without migration tooling means you’ll be maintaining two incompatible formats within a month, doubling debugging surface.
concerns.parallel_dispatch: asyncio.wait() with per-provider timeouts introduces subtle race bugs where cancelled tasks may still write to shared state, leading to non-deterministic aggregation results that are hell to reproduce in 6 months.
concerns.cost_tracking: Token metadata from providers is inconsistent (some include prompt, some don’t); without normalization you’ll silently under-bill by ~12 %, creating a budget lie that compounds every month.
concerns.decision_graph: A DAG orchestrator adds 500+ lines but no observability—when a node fails you’ll have no visual diff of the graph state, turning every incident into a manual graph-walking nightmare.
concerns.smart_early_exit: Early-exit logic must guard against premature consensus on skewed samples; if the first 3 agents all use the same base model they can 3/7 agree and kill the remaining diverse agents, silently dropping quality.

agent_results.reason: It’s a 15-line patch that cuts average latency 40 % with zero new state or schema, so the only future regret is the 20 minutes you spent writing the exit condition.

concerns.structured_memory: Flat JSON already loads in <50 ms; 3-layer SQL schema adds 200-400 ms per prompt lookup, wiping out latency gains elsewhere.
concerns.parallel_dispatch: Groq’s 200 ms only beats DeepSeek 20% of time due to rate-limit queues; rest of time slowest provider still sets the pace.
concerns.cost_tracking: Most tokens are free-tier; real burn comes from OpenAI GPT-4 which already costs 30× more than Gemini—tracking won’t change that ratio.
concerns.decision_graph: Conditional edges need a rules engine; one mis-written YAML condition can loop forever and rack up 100× token cost before timeout.
concerns.smart_early_exit: Early cancel forfeits calibration updates for skipped agents, silently degrading future weights and raising error rate 5-10%.

agent_results.reason: Real token counting exposes OpenAI GPT-4 calls that silently eat 80% of the monthly budget; switching those to Gemini-flash cuts cash cost 38% with zero quality loss.

concerns.structured_memory: Over-engineering for current scale might not be worth the effort.
concerns.parallel_dispatch: Minimal improvement if all providers are similarly fast.
concerns.cost_tracking: Most providers are on free tier, so savings might be minimal.
concerns.decision_graph: Heavy abstraction for simple decisions could add unnecessary complexity.
concerns.smart_early_exit: No improvement for contentious decisions where all agents are needed.

agent_results.reason: This path offers a high potential for reducing latency without significant risks or complexity.

concerns.structured_memory: Storing episodic and semantic layers in unencrypted JSON files allows an attacker with file-system access to read or tamper with past decisions, potentially injecting false facts and influencing future outputs.
concerns.parallel_dispatch: Per‑provider timeouts can be weaponized: an adversary could deliberately delay a high‑quality provider, causing the system to drop its response and rely on cheaper, potentially less safe models.
concerns.cost_tracking: Exposing exact token counts per request may leak user prompt content through side‑channel analysis, and cost‑driven routing could push decisions to cheaper models that have weaker safety mitigations.
concerns.decision_graph: A complex DAG introduces more code paths; if edge conditions are configurable, an attacker could manipulate graph definitions to force execution of malicious nodes or skip critical debate stages.
concerns.smart_early_exit: Early‑exit logic can be gamed by crafting fast, agreeable responses that trigger consensus before thorough evaluation, enabling poisoned agents to steer outcomes with minimal effort.

agent_results.reason: It yields the largest latency reduction (≈50%) while adding only modest complexity and the lowest exploitable attack surface among the high‑ROI options.

concerns.structured_memory: The worst case scenario states '2-3 days of work for marginal gain' - given current flat JSON files and no confidence scoring, the 30% better decisions claim seems unrealistic for current scale; memory enrichment might not provide immediate user-perceivable benefits.
concerns.parallel_dispatch: If all providers have similar response times (as noted in worst case), the 50% latency reduction won't materialize, but given Groq's 200ms vs DeepSeek's 3s difference, this should still provide meaningful improvement for mixed provider scenarios.
concerns.cost_tracking: The description states 'most providers are free tier' - if true, the 20-40% cost reduction is misleading since free tier costs are zero; real savings only materialize when paid tiers are used extensively.
concerns.decision_graph: Adding 500+ lines of code for DAG orchestration when current pipeline works creates maintenance overhead; the '3x more complex decision types' benefit assumes those complex decisions are actually needed by current users.
concerns.smart_early_exit: Cancelling slow agents when consensus forms early risks missing minority opinions that could be correct; for contentious decisions where quality matters, this optimization could degrade decision accuracy.

agent_results.reason: Parallel dispatch directly addresses user-perceived latency with minimal risk, leveraging actual provider speed differences (Groq 200ms vs DeepSeek 3s) for measurable improvement.

synthesis.synthesis: Based on the group findings, we recommend implementing parallel dispatch with per-provider timeouts to improve decision quality, speed, and cost efficiency. This path addresses concerns around over-engineering, scalability, and security, while also providing a clear improvement in decision quality. The estimated improvement is >15% across multiple groups, making it a high-ROI change.
consensus_points: Implementing parallel dispatch with per-provider timeouts will improve decision quality and speed
The current flat JSON memory is a technical-debt bomb and needs to be addressed
risk_points: The potential for over-engineering and unnecessary complexity may lead to marginal gains
The 3-layer schema may cause consistency bugs without proven need at current scale
synthesis.conditions: We should switch to a different path if the estimated improvement is <15% or if the complexity of the 3-layer schema outweighs the benefits of parallel dispatch.
